examples.title: Safety Algorithm
examples.description: Determines if system is in safe state and finds safe sequence
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Safety
INPUT: Available[], Allocation[][], Need[][]
OUTPUT: SafeSequence[], Safe/Unsafe status

BEGIN
  WORK = Available[]
  FINISH[n] = FALSE for all processes
  SAFE_SEQUENCE = empty
  
  FOR i = 1 to n DO
    FOUND = FALSE
    FOR j = 1 to n DO
      IF FINISH[j] = FALSE AND Need[j] <= WORK THEN
        WORK = WORK + Allocation[j]
        FINISH[j] = TRUE
        ADD j to SAFE_SEQUENCE
        FOUND = TRUE
      END IF
    END FOR
    IF FOUND = FALSE THEN
      BREAK
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  IF all FINISH[i] = TRUE THEN
    RETURN "SAFE" with SAFE_SEQUENCE
  ELSE
    RETURN "UNSAFE"
  END IF
END
examples.explanation: The safety algorithm simulates resource allocation to find a safe sequence. It repeatedly finds processes whose needs can be satisfied with available resources, completes them, and releases their resources back.

examples.title: Banker's Algorithm Implementation
examples.description: Complete implementation of Banker's Algorithm with resource request
examples.code:
def is_safe(processes, avail, max_mat, alloc):
    n = len(processes)
    m = len(avail)
    
    # Calculate Need matrix
    need = [[max_mat[i][j] - alloc[i][j] for j in range(m)] for i in range(n)]
    
    work = avail[:]
    finish = [False] * n
    safe_seq = []
    
    for _ in range(n):
        found = False
        for i in range(n):
            if not finish[i] and all(need[i][j] <= work[j] for j in range(m)):
                for j in range(m):
                    work[j] += alloc[i][j]
                finish[i] = True
                safe_seq.append(processes[i])
                found = True
        if not found:
            return False, []
    
    return True, safe_seq

def request_resource(process_id, request, processes, avail, max_mat, alloc, need):
    m = len(avail)
    
    # Check if request <= need
    if any(request[j] > need[process_id][j] for j in range(m)):
        return "Error: Process exceeds maximum claim"
    
    # Check if request <= available
    if any(request[j] > avail[j] for j in range(m)):
        return "Process must wait"
    
    # Pretend to allocate
    for j in range(m):
        avail[j] -= request[j]
        alloc[process_id][j] += request[j]
        need[process_id][j] -= request[j]
    
    # Check safety
    safe, seq = is_safe(processes, avail, max_mat, alloc)
    
    if safe:
        return f"Request granted. Safe sequence: {seq}"
    else:
        # Rollback
        for j in range(m):
            avail[j] += request[j]
            alloc[process_id][j] -= request[j]
            need[process_id][j] += request[j]
        return "Request denied - would lead to unsafe state"
examples.explanation: Python implementation showing safety check and resource request handling. The request_resource function pretends to allocate, checks safety, and rolls back if unsafe.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n² × m) for safety, O(n × m) for request
